quail_location_and_history.quail_once_lived_here:
This location is an old family hunting spot in the lower foothills of the southern Sierra Nevada, on the west slope above the Kern River Valley. My father first took me there in the late 1970s when I was a teenager, and he said he had hunted Valley Quail in the same draws and ridges with his own father starting in the late 1950s. The area is a patchwork of BLM land and private cattle ranches, with rolling oak savanna transitioning into chaparral-covered slopes.

Historically, coveys of California (Valley) Quail were consistently found along the small seasonal drainages and around a couple of old stock tanks that held water into late summer. In good years, we would routinely flush multiple coveys in a single morning, sometimes 40–60 birds total over the course of a day’s hunt. The birds used the dense riparian vegetation in the drainages and the scattered buckbrush, manzanita, and chamise on the slopes for cover, moving up and down with the time of day and temperature.

Over the last 20–25 years, I have noticed a steady decline. By the early 2000s, we were seeing smaller coveys, and by around 2010, it became common to hunt all morning and see only a handful of birds, if any. In the past 8–10 years, I have not personally flushed a covey here, and recent trips have been quiet except for a few songbirds and the occasional rabbit. The habitat has become noticeably drier, with more bare ground, more invasive grasses like red brome and cheatgrass, and fewer green understory plants in late spring. Several of the small seeps and the main stock pond that used to be reliable are now dry by early summer and sometimes never fill at all in low-rainfall winters.

This location is important to me personally because it is where I learned to identify quail calls at dawn, to read tracks in the dust, and to safely hunt with family and close friends. It is also one of the clearest examples, in my own lifetime, of how a once-productive quail spot can essentially go silent as water sources and vegetative cover decline.
quail_location_and_history.nearest_city_or_landmark: Northeast of Lake Isabella, roughly 6–8 miles east of Woody, CA
quail_location_and_history.general_description_of_area:
The area is a series of low to mid-elevation foothills (approximately 2,000–3,000 feet elevation) on the western slope of the southern Sierra Nevada, north and slightly east of Bakersfield and a bit west of Lake Isabella. The terrain consists of rolling to moderately steep hills cut by small, mostly seasonal drainages that flow only during and shortly after winter and early spring storms.

Vegetation historically was dominated by blue oak and interior live oak scattered across open grassy slopes, with denser stands of oak and willow along the creek bottoms. Understory and shrub species include buckbrush (Ceanothus cuneatus), chamise, manzanita, and some scattered sage and rabbitbrush. The ground layer used to have a mix of native and non-native grasses and forbs, but in the last couple of decades, invasive annual grasses like red brome and cheatgrass have become much more dominant. There are also patches of star thistle and other invasive weeds in disturbed areas, especially near old ranch roads and around the former stock tanks.

The land is partially grazed by cattle, and you can see old barbed-wire fences, rusted gates, and remnants of wooden corrals. Several dirt two-tracks and ranch roads provide access to the ridges and drainages. Historically, there were a couple of small earthen stock ponds and at least one older concrete watering trough fed by a spring or developed seep.

Winters can be cool and wet in good rainfall years, but in the last 10–15 years, precipitation has been erratic, with several multi-year drought periods. Summers are hot and dry, often with extended stretches of triple-digit temperatures down in the valley and very warm conditions in the foothills. The combination of drought, heavy grazing in some areas, and likely increased evaporation has visibly reduced the amount of green cover and the persistence of surface water into late spring and summer.

In terms of wildlife, we used to see quail, mule deer, cottontails, jackrabbits, coyotes, and occasional bobcats. Now we still see some deer and coyotes, but the conspicuous absence is the sound of quail — very few calls at dawn, and almost no covey flushes, even when walking the same ridges and drainages that were once reliable.
quail_location_and_history.last_years_birds_were_seen: Personally last seen in small numbers around 2013–2015; my father recalls reliable coveys through the late 1990s.
quail_location_and_history.gps_coordinates: Approximate center of historic hunting area: 35.6940° N, 118.8095° W (WGS84). This is not a precise single point, but represents the general cluster of drainages and ridges we hunted northeast of Woody and west of Lake Isabella.
quail_location_and_history.usgs_7_1_2_minute_map_name: WOODY, CA 7.5-minute quadrangle (adjacent to LAKE ISABELLA SOUTH quadrangle)

nearest_historic_water_source.name_of_water_source: Old North Ridge Stock Tank (local family nickname; not an official name)
nearest_historic_water_source.location_description_and_or_gps_coordina:
The primary historic water source we relied on was an earthen stock pond locals sometimes referred to as the "North Ridge Tank." It sits in a shallow saddle just below the crest of a north–south running ridge, catching runoff from two small draws that funnel into it from the east and west.

Approximate coordinates for the center of the old tank basin are 35.6968° N, 118.8042° W (WGS84). The tank is roughly 3/4 mile east-northeast of the small dirt two-track that branches off the main ranch road coming up from the Woody area. From the ridge, you can see down toward the Kern River Valley on clear days, and on the opposite side you look toward the Lake Isabella basin.

The tank itself was an excavated depression with a low earthen berm on the downhill side. When I was a kid in the late 1970s and early 1980s, it usually held water from late winter through at least mid- to late summer in average rainfall years. By late August or September, the water level would drop but there was typically still a muddy edge with enough moisture for quail, deer, and cattle to drink. We would often find quail tracks in the damp mud along the shaded edge, and it was a reliable place to hear coveys calling at first light.

There was no windmill or permanent concrete trough at this particular tank, just the earthen basin, some trampled edges from cattle, and a few scattered oaks and shrubs providing partial shade. Over time, the berm has eroded, the basin has partially filled in with silt and invasive grasses, and in recent drought years it has not held visible surface water at all.

Nearby landmarks include an old rusted-out water truck carcass about 150 yards to the southeast and a collapsed wooden loading chute downhill to the southwest. These features are still visible and help confirm that this is the same watering point my father and grandfather used to talk about.
nearest_historic_water_source.approximate_distance_from_quail: The stock tank lies approximately 0.6–0.8 miles (about 1.0–1.3 kilometers) from the core of our historic hunting area, measured along the ridgeline and down into the main drainage where we used to encounter the largest coveys. In a straight line (as the crow flies), the distance from the center of our most frequently hunted drainage to the tank is roughly 0.5 miles (0.8 km). Quail would typically move between the tank and the adjacent brushy slopes in the early morning and late afternoon, using the small draws and scattered shrubs as cover.
nearest_historic_water_source.if_no_what_was_the_last_year_it_was_know:
The last time I personally observed standing water in the North Ridge Tank was during the late winter/early spring of 2011, following a relatively decent rainfall season. Even then, the pond was noticeably shallower and did not appear to hold water as late into the summer as it had in the 1980s and 1990s.

My father recalls that in the early 2000s (around 2002–2004) the tank still regularly held water into mid-summer in good years, but by the late 2000s it was often dry by early summer. Since approximately 2013, every time I have checked—usually once or twice a year during the cooler months—the basin has been completely dry or at most slightly damp after a storm, with no persistent standing water.

If I had to identify a single year as the last clearly reliable year, I would say 2011 was the last year it was known to consistently have water for more than a few weeks. After that, any water present was short-lived and not dependable for quail or livestock, and by the mid-2010s the tank functionally ceased to be a usable water source.
